The Universal Self-Consistency Framework (USCF) introduces a meta-theoretical architecture designed to bound physical law across quantum, relativistic, and cosmological scales. Rather than proposing a new "Theory of Everything," USCF defines a fundamental principle: all physical evolution must preserve global self-consistency across geometry, energy, and information. Through rigorous stress testing in extreme regimes-including black hole interiors, early-universe inflation, and Planck-scale limits-this framework demonstrates that singularities are not physical endpoints but phase transitions.

The coefficient of determination R 2 quantifies the amount of variance explained by regression coefficients in a linear model. It can be seen as the fixed-effects complement to the repeatability R (intra-class correlation) for the variance explained by random effects and thus as a tool for variance decomposition. Abstract Constructing efficient catalysts for the N 2 reduction reaction (NRR) is a major challenge for artificial nitrogen fixation under ambient conditions. Herein, inspired by the principle of “like dissolves like”, it is demonstrated that a member of the nitrogen family, well‐exfoliated few‐layer black phosphorus nanosheets (FL‐BP NSs), can be used as an efficient nonmetallic catalyst for electrochemical nitrogen reduction. The catalyst can achieve a high ammonia yield of 31.37 μg h −1 mg −1 cat.
